seoTitle: How to Match Image Sizes in Google Docs (2026)
metaDescription: Learn how to standardize picture sizes in Google Docs using the Image Options sidebar and the Paint Format tool to copy dimensions quickly.
intro: Uneven image sizes can throw off the visual balance of a report or portfolio. This guide covers matching dimensions manually through the Image Options sidebar, as well as a faster method using Paint Format to copy sizing from one image to another. It's for anyone cleaning up a document with multiple photos or graphics.

steps.heading: Select your first image
steps.body: Click the first image in your document, right-click it, and choose Image Options to open the sidebar.

steps.heading: Lock the aspect ratio and set a size
steps.body: Check the Lock Aspect Ratio box, then type an exact target width value into the sidebar field.

steps.heading: Repeat for each additional image
steps.body: Select your next image and enter the same numerical width value to match the first.

steps.heading: Use Paint Format for speed
steps.body: Select your correctly sized master image, click the Paint Format icon, then click a misaligned image to instantly copy the matching dimensions.

steps.heading: Check text wrap and alignment
steps.body: Adjust each image's Wrap Text or In Line setting to keep your images aligned in a clean, symmetrical grid.

faq.question: Why should I lock the aspect ratio?
faq.answer: Locking the aspect ratio prevents images from stretching or distorting when you resize them.

faq.question: What's the fastest way to match many images at once?
faq.answer: The Paint Format tool lets you copy a master image's sizing and paste it onto other images with a single click each.

faq.question: Can I match height instead of width?
faq.answer: Yes, the Image Options sidebar lets you set either dimension, and with aspect ratio locked, the other adjusts proportionally.

faq.question: Does resizing affect image quality?
faq.answer: Resizing within reasonable limits generally preserves quality, but significantly enlarging a small image can cause pixelation.
